fields.Text:

{{c1::Lye ingestion}}

fields.Text:
Cold pulseless leg with preserved sensation/motor function and angiographic occlusion. Best initial therapy?

{{c1::Catheter-directed thrombolysis}}
fields.Extra:
Q12. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Digital subtraction angiography guides catheter-directed thrombolysis and monitors clot dissolution. Successful thrombolysis only restores baseline flow, requiring additional intervention to treat underlying stenosis, and prevent re-occlusion. Inflow disease should be corrected before initiating thrombolysis.

fields.Text:
HIDA scan with nonvisualized gallbladder after 60 minutes. Next step?

{{c1::IV morphine and continued imaging}}
fields.Extra:
Q15. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Non-visualization of the gallbladder on HIDA scan with normal liver-to-bowel transit suggests acute cholecystitis. Morphine sulfate administration improves diagnostic specificity by increasing biliary pressure—normal gallbladders will fill while obstructed ones remain non-visualized, confirming cystic duct obstruction.

fields.Text:
Bilateral symmetric parietal bone thinning. Most associated systemic condition?

{{c1::Osteoporosis}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q17. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Biparietal osteodystrophy represents a regional manifestation of systemic osteoporotic bone loss, where disrupted bone metabolism becomes clinically apparent in the vulnerable parietal cortical bone. The condition should prompt comprehensive evaluation for underlying osteoporosis and associated metabolic bone disorders requiring systemic treatment.

fields.Text: An I-123 MIBG scan localizes catecholamine-secreting tumors such as {{c1::pheochromocytoma}}.
fields.Extra:
Q18. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Approximately 10% of pheochromocytomas are bilateral or metastatic, so an I-123 MIBG or gallium-68 DOTATATE scan is needed to identify any additional lesions. While a CT scan can identify an adrenal lesion, it cannot assess functionality or locate metastatic pheochromocytomas as effectively as a radioisotope scan.




Open review Q18

fields.Text:
Left hepatic lobe MR ghosting/blurring adjacent to the aorta. Artifact?

{{c1::Pulsation artifact}}
fields.Extra:
Q19. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Pulsation artifacts always propagate in the phase-encoding direction, creating ghost copies of moving structures (like the aorta) that appear as faint replicas at regular intervals. This is because phase-encoding is the slowest part of MRI data acquisition, making it most susceptible to motion corruption, while frequency-encoding occurs rapidly and is relatively motion-resistant.




Open review Q19

fields.Text: Aortic pulsation can create {{c1::pulsation artifact}} in the adjacent left hepatic lobe on MRI.
fields.Extra:

This is because phase-encoding is the slowest part of MRI data acquisition, making it most susceptible to motion corruption, while frequency-encoding occurs rapidly and is relatively motion-resistant.





Open review Q19

fields.Text: Continuous-wave Doppler requires {{c1::two crystals}} because it transmits and receives simultaneously.
fields.Extra:
Q20. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Continuous wave Doppler uses two separate crystals within the probe. One crystal is constantly transmitting while the second is continuously recording.

fields.Text:
Facial trauma with horizontal maxillary fracture pattern. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::LeFort I fracture}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q27. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: In a LeFort 1 fracture, there is no superior extension through the orbital floor (which is seen in LeFort 2) or through the medial and lateral orbits and zygomatic arches (as is seen in LeFort 3). On physical exam, the LeFort 1 fracture will demonstrate mobility of the maxillary teeth and palate but not of the nose or zygomas. Multiple LeFort fractures may coexist and the Lefort level may not be symmetric (e.g., a patient may have LeFort 1 and 2 on the right and LeFort 1, 2, and 3 on the left).




Open review Q27

fields.Text: LeFort I fracture separates the {{c1::maxilla/hard palate from the midface}} in a horizontal fracture plane.

On physical exam, the LeFort 1 fracture will demonstrate mobility of the maxillary teeth and palate but not of the nose or zygomas. Multiple LeFort fractures may coexist and the Lefort level may not be symmetric (e.g., a patient may have LeFort 1 and 2 on the right and LeFort 1, 2, and 3 on the left).

fields.Text: Cancer is a stochastic radiation effect because its probability increases with dose but {{c1::severity is not dose-dependent}}.
fields.Extra:
Q28. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Stochastic effects follow the linear no-threshold model where any radiation dose carries cancer risk with probability increasing linearly with dose but severity remaining constant, contrasting with deterministic effects that require threshold doses and show dose-dependent severity.

{{c1::Balloon angioplasty}}

Postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial mass. Key risk factor group?

{{c1::Endometrial carcinoma risk factors}}

Q33. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Endometrial cancer demonstrates intermediate-to-low T2 signal intensity relative to hyperintense normal endometrium, with restricted diffusion and hypoenhancement on contrast-enhanced imaging. Risk factors include obesity, diabetes, unopposed estrogen exposure, and Lynch syndrome.

fields.Text:
MRI with central abdominal signal loss at high field strength. Artifact?

{{c1::Dielectric artifact}}
fields.Extra:
Q34. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Dielectric effect results from central signal loss at 3 T when radiofrequency wavelength approximates patient diameter, creating standing wave interference that prevents central spin excitation. The artifact can be reduced by using dielectric pads between patient and coil, or by preferentially imaging large patients, pregnant patients, and those with ascites at 1.5 T instead of 3 T.




Open review Q34

fields.Text: Dielectric artifact causes {{c1::central signal loss/shading}} on MRI, especially at higher field strength.
fields.Extra:
Q34. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Dielectric effect results from central signal loss at 3 T when radiofrequency wavelength approximates patient diameter, creating standing wave interference that prevents central spin excitation. The artifact can be reduced by using dielectric pads between patient and coil, or by preferentially imaging large patients, pregnant patients, and those with ascites at 1.5 T instead of 3 T.





Open review Q34

fields.Text: As kVp increases, the relative proportion of {{c1::Compton scatter increases compared with photoelectric effect}}.
fields.Extra:
Q35. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Photoelectric effect's cubic energy dependence (E³) makes it extremely sensitive to kVp changes, while Compton scattering's linear energy dependence (E¹) creates much more gradual changes, causing dramatic interaction ratio shifts with small kVp adjustments in diagnostic energy ranges.

{{c1::Damaged Faraday cage}}

fields.Text: Zipper artifact is a linear band of noise usually caused by {{c1::radiofrequency leakage from a damaged Faraday cage}}.

Treated cerebellar toxoplasmosis followed by crossed cerebral peduncle signal change. Mechanism?

{{c1::Transneuronal degeneration}}
fields.Extra:
Q43. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Right hypertrophic olivary degeneration with left cerebellar abscess follows the contralateral pattern - a left cerebellar lesion disrupts the left dentate nucleus, causing deafferentiation and subsequent hypertrophy of the contralateral (right) inferior olivary nucleus via interruption of the dentate-rubro-olivary pathway.

fields.Text: An anomalous right coronary artery can arise from the {{c1::left coronary sinus of Valsalva}}.
fields.Extra:
Q44. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Anomalous RCA arising from the left coronary cusp requires immediate assessment of arterial course—an inter-arterial path (between aorta and pulmonary artery) carries high risk of sudden cardiac death in young athletes due to potential compression during exercise, while prepulmonic, retroaortic, or septal courses are benign variants requiring no intervention.





Open review Q44

fields.Text:
Pediatric posterior tibial exostosis with cartilage cap. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Osteochondroma}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: hard. The T2 weighted images show posterior exostosis with T2 cap signal.




Open review Q45

fields.Text: Osteochondroma is an exostosis with {{c1::cortical and medullary continuity with the parent bone}} and a cartilage cap.
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: hard. The T2 weighted images show posterior exostosis with T2 cap signal.






Open review Q45

fields.Text:
Adnexal structures on transvaginal US. Best maneuver to determine whether they arise separately?

{{c1::Apply gentle pressure with the transvaginal probe}}
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: not listed. When pressure is applied between the two left adnexal structures during real-time scanning, they will separate if they are arising from different anatomic location (as in the case of left tubal ectopic with left ovarian cystic lesion, as shown here).




Open review Q46

fields.Text: On transvaginal US, gentle probe pressure can separate adnexal structures if they arise from {{c1::different anatomic locations}}.
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: not listed. When pressure is applied between the two left adnexal structures during real-time scanning, they will separate if they are arising from different anatomic location (as in the case of left tubal ectopic with left ovarian cystic lesion, as shown here).





Open review Q46

fields.Text: Atypical ductal hyperplasia on core biopsy is treated with {{c1::surgical excision}} because of upgrade risk to DCIS.
fields.Extra:
Q47. Correct; difficulty: easy. Atypical ductal hyperplasia (ADH) should under surgical excision as a significant portion are upstaged to ductal carcinoma in situ on final pathology.




Open review Q47

fields.Text: A solitary solid pulmonary nodule greater than 8 mm can be followed initially with CT at {{c1::6-12 months and again at 18-24 months}} if surveillance is chosen.
fields.Extra:
Q48. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Fleischner Society guidelines use risk-stratified management where patient factors (age, smoking history) combined with nodule size determine follow-up intervals more than morphologic features. For 6 to 8 mm solid nodules in high-risk patients, systematic follow-up at 6 to 12 months then 18 to 24 months is recommended regardless of smooth margins, as benign morphology does not exclude malignancy in high-risk individuals.




Open review Q48

fields.Text: Researching a new tracer falls under the professional responsibility of {{c1::scientific knowledge}}.
fields.Extra:
Q49. Correct; difficulty: easy. The radiologist’s research signifies the quest for scientific knowledge, which is a key professional responsibility.




Open review Q49

fields.Text: For an NRC reportable medical event, the NRC must be notified by telephone within {{c1::24 hours}}.
fields.Extra:
Q50. Correct; difficulty: not listed. For all medical uses of NRC-licensed radioactive materials, a "medical event" occurs if both the dose administered differs from what was prescribed and the difference meets the NRC's reporting requirements, and one or more of the following representative incidents occur: 1.
